title: Sketching Signals
summary: This page explains the concept of the time constant for decaying exponential signals, defining it as the time interval over which the exponential drops to 1/e (approximately 37%) of its value. It demonstrates how to use the time constant to quickly sketch exponential functions, using e^{-2t} as a worked example with a time constant of 0.5 seconds. Figures illustrate both general monotonic exponentials and specific sketches of e^{-at} and e^{-2t}.

figures.caption: Sketching (a) e^{-at}u(t) and (b) e^{-2t}u(t), illustrating the time constant and key amplitude values at 0.37 and 0.135.
figures.description: The figure shows two coordinate plots with horizontal axis t and vertical amplitude, sketching the decaying exponentials e^{-at}u(t) and e^{-2t}u(t). In both plots the curve starts at value 1 at t=0 and decreases monotonically toward 0 for t>0, with labeled reference points marking 1/e ≈ 0.37 and 1/e^2 ≈ 0.135. The left plot labels these at t=1/a and t=2/a, while the right plot gives the specific case a=2 with the same amplitudes at t=0.5 and t=1. Students should notice the time constant relationship: after one time constant the exponential falls to about 37% of its initial value, and after two time constants to about 13.5%.
